This is a cleaner way to do it.  BaseExceptions should generally not be caught.  The need to catch `CancelledError` and re-raise it is because it was an `Exception` in 3.7 and promoted to a `BaseException` only in 3.8.  So, for 3.7 we must make sure it isn't handled with the more general `Exception`.

Thank you, @sairina . The overall direction is looking great. I have two high-level suggestions for now that I think would be good to address.

For later reference, the  `NotificationsRoot` is visually responsible for the following four components which I'll call the messaging components

| `AuthMessage` | `AppError` | `GlobalSnackbar` | `UpdateNotification` |
| -------------------- | -------------- | ----------------------- | --------------------------- |
| ![AuthMessage](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/13509191/156009457-d918fbe9-0ceb-40b8-8281-4d495788c9f1.png) | ![AppError](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/13509191/156009448-ca417992-744e-4af2-97e9-5aa9c9aa62d3.png)  | ![GlobalSnackbar](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/13509191/156009461-6488be67-7021-485d-9197-443deda639eb.png) | ![UpdateNotification](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/13509191/156009462-9a5f0118-dc6f-4e3f-8f61-925b75d1c307.png) |

### (1) Decoupling of unnecessary style and layout information (including deletion of related props)

 I’d suggest removing all styles that are not directly related to the messaging components. That’s the majority of computed styles (e.g. `mainWrapperStyles` , `contentStyles`) as well as styles defined in `<style>` block. It’d be fine to keep styles that are unnecessary to position these components on a page if there are some. This also applies to related props: `fullScreen` , `hasSidebar` ,`maxMainWidth` , and `marginBottom`.

**Reason**

`NotificationsRoot` is planned to be used in the following way from plugin index pages:

```javascript
// LearnIndex

<template>
  <NotificationsRoot>
    <router-view />
  </NotificationsRoot>
</template>
```

One of the points of `CoreBase` refactor is to allow each page of the plugin that’s rendered in the `<router-view />` to have full control over its layout, including margins, padding, etc. to gain more flexibility. That wouldn’t be fully possible if we kept unnecessary layout and style dependencies in the `NotificationsRoot`.

### (2) Decoupling loading state from the default slot

I’d suggest removing  `v-if="!loading"` from [the whole wrapper div here](https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/pull/9128/files#diff-4a205996a86d148f2aeb0e7a84a2f6ea6325fe25f0f548eae3230a4390abdce4R9) and only using it directly on components that need it, that’s `AuthMessage` and `AppError` . For example

```javascript
<KPageContainer v-if="!loading && notAuthorized">
  <AuthMessage
    :authorizedRole="authorizedRole"
    :header="authorizationErrorHeader"
    :details="authorizationErrorDetails"
  />
</KPageContainer>
```

**Reason**

Keeping `v-if="!loading"` on the div that’s also wrapping [the default slot](https://github.com/learningequality/kolibri/pull/9128/files#diff-4a205996a86d148f2aeb0e7a84a2f6ea6325fe25f0f548eae3230a4390abdce4R33) has unhappy consequences. Consider the situation demonstrated in the following example when we’d try to set loading state from one of the pages of the Learn plugin:

```javascript
// LearnIndex

<template>
  <NotificationsRoot>
    <router-view />
  </NotificationsRoot>
</template>
```

```javascript
// Library (rendered within the <router-view /> of LearnIndex)

<script>

export default {
  created() {
    state.loading = true
    fetchData().then(() => state.loading = false)
  }
}

</script>
```

At the moment we set `state.loading = true` in the `created` hook, the Library component would get destroyed (therefore `state.loading = false` would never get called) if we kept `v-if="!loading"` as is in the `NotificationsRoot` . This would make all logic around the loading state rigid and difficult to refactor later (and debug - true story :)

---

To sum it up, this means we’d only keep things that are absolutely unnecessary for notifications and messaging components in the `NotificationsRoot`  (and if we find out we need something more from it in the future, we can do that when a need arises while considering architecture and alternative options to solving that particular need).

If I understand correctly, the behavior change here is that we now create the new `server_call` _after_ the reply callback has been called, where previously we were creating the new call _before_ even invoking the RPC handler. Please correct me if I'm wrong.

What will happen if we drain the queue of `server_call`s in this case (e.g., we have a buffer of 100 `server_call`s and we have 100 RPCs that are all outstanding)? I would assume that this would block the RPC from being handled until one of the outstanding RPCs finishes. This may cause problems in the case of e.g., asyncio actors where there may be thousands of outstanding actor calls concurrently. If that's the case, we will probably need to either (1) warn users that there's a max number of outstanding actor calls and probably default to something quite high or (2) make the limit configurable.

If we're going to merge this, we should definitely run the full suite of nightly/release benchmarks and stress tests on it. A good start would be to post the microbenchmark results before and after the PR.

I think it would be worthwhile adding a code comment here that explains that c.session.Accept() blocks until c.session.Close() is closed, which happens in c.Close(), but c.Close() also locks c.m, so we have to release it while c.session.Accept() runs, in order to avoid the deadlock. We could mention that this deadlock was first discovered when https://go-review.googlesource.com/c/go/+/409537 started enforcing that listener go routines complete before active connections are cleaned up after a server close / shutdown, and that prior to this, this deadlock occurred, but was not detected, leaving zombie go routines in a deadlocked state.

There is quite a lot going on in spead2 and I cant pretend that all of it is clear to me. I have looked over the main changes and most of it seems to be in order. In summary, the main changes introduced in this pull request are:
1. Added logic to simplify heap assembly when packets are expected to be received in order.
2. Streamlined the stream configuration option to two objects: a StreamConfig() and RingStreamConfig() object

I also spent some time looking at the examples and the documentation. I have some thoughts on what could be added there. They are not related to the changes made in this review, so consider them as suggestions.
1. When reading the documentation for the first time, it is difficult to figure out how one would start a SPEAD2 project. The documentation contains a detailed API, but it requires a bit of legwork to figure out how all the different bits fit together(eg: You know you have a memory pool and a thread pool, but then you need to go over the rest of the docs to figure out where they fit in). The examples in spead2/examples serve to show how all the parts interact, but I could not find any reference to these examples in the docs. If you install spead2 using "pip install", then it is quite likely that you wont even know that the examples exist. To make this more accessible to first time users, I would recommend explicitly mentioning the examples somewhere in the documentation.
2. There is a test_send_asyncio.py script in the examples. Is it worth having a test_receive_asyncio.py script? In recv.py in katfgpu there is a AsyncRingbuffer class. Was this specific to katfgpu or would this be something someone using standard spead2 would need to consider?

Thanks @Irving-cl for the update. Looks much nicer.

Some smaller comments/suggestion below.

The main suggestion/point from me still is about parsing of TLV/sub-TLVs. I think it's important to write the parsing of TLV/sub-TLVs in a way that it stays compatible with any future extensions/changes (e.g. if new sub-TLVs are added or new fields are added in an existing TLV). We take care of this in other parts of OT dealing with TLVs (e.g., the network-data TLVs and subTLVs).

In the new link-metrics code, we have some hard-coded assumptions:
- Some of the new TLV code assumes the TLV is valid if its length is smaller than some min expected value. Generally it should be the other way round, i.e. a larger TLV should be considered valid (to allow for future additions to TLV definition).
- Parsing over sub-TLVs, the code currently assume certain order and exact number of sub-TLVs to be present, it should be flexible and skip over any sub-TLVs that it does not know/support.

I tried providing some examples of existing/related code where we have similar patterns to show approaches/solution on how to handle them.

> Does rust support utf-8 in source code?

Yep! Unicode is permitted in source code files, and moreover it is
[permitted in identifiers][play]:

```rust
let 유니코드 = true;
assert!(유니코드);
```

[play]: https://play.rust-lang.org/?version=nightly&mode=debug&edition=2018&gist=132fb2ac089e96cc383164029dc8d0cd

> Can I write a macro that aliases `clap` to  ? :D

Not quite. You can use ~arbitrary Unicode in string literals, comments,
etc., but identifiers must meet the [Unicode identifier contraints][s].
This includes basically “things that appear in words in human
languages”, but does not include emoji.

[s]: http://www.unicode.org/reports/tr31/#Default_Identifier_Syntax

I had a couple small suggestions and then I have one big one that I would like tested out either in this PR or a promise that it will be done in a future PR. Right now the general flow of the dask arrays goes like this:

```
data -> delayed(_sort_weights) -> indexing and linspace -> delayed(_get_bin_statistics) -> reshape
```

To me there isn't much benefit to having that middle indexing and linspace section of code. The only benefit I see is if the indexing is much faster when done in parallel with dask, but since the indexes are coming from a delayed function (no real chunking) and it is then fed into the second delayed function right away. I think all this logic should be one giant delayed function. It'd be nice if it could be done with `map_blocks` but I don't see a way to do that. The only reason not to do this would be if combining all of these resulted in a large memory usage because all of the temporary arrays (`del idxs_sorted` might help with that).

Lastly, while looking at how `self.idxs` is generated, I think that entire function (`_get_indices`) should be turned into a map_blocks call. There are a lot of little intermediate operations there that are just adding more overhead to the dask graph. However, now that I say that I see that `y_idxs`, `x_idxs`, and `idxs` are all assigned to `self` in this method so maybe it isn't as pretty as I had hoped. Wait! No, `y_idxs` and `x_idxs` are never used outside this method, right? Having a `map_blocks` function that took `lons` and `lats` as inputs and returned the equivalent of `self.idxs` should clean up the dask graph a bit and I would hope improve memory usage and maybe execution time. Bare minimum the dask graph would look better.

def log_invocation(fn):

sorry, but I do not really understand what pain point this is trying to address. the container is not built from source, and it is developing in an arm64 linux container (which runs inside a linux vm), which is an environment that no one really uses today (and we also have almost no tests running on arm64 linux platforms).

note that, the requirements and dependencies are checked in inside the repo today, and gets updated like every week by people. so if the dev container here is not able to pick the dependencies from the repo, I do not think it can really claim that "this provides a hermetic environment with all needed dependencies.".

also `ray-deps` do not have ml dependencies, it is just a `base-deps` with a wheel copied in. so the use seems to be a bit limited.

I feel that one can just create an arm64 graviton linux ec2 vm (or x86), and achieve the approximately same thing with vscode+ssh, and even without containers (which is basically the workflow I uses today).

maybe it makes linux local dev easier, but I worry about the false signals that it might provide. because the platform it uses is the least used one.

constructively, here are some points for improvement:

- if the purpose is to test with the dependencies, to make this useful, I think this should be building from source, ideally without adding new `Dockerfile` that is only used for dev.
- ray supposedly also supports osx and apple silicon, so if the purpose is to test ray library logic, instead of using a container, maybe we should get the apple silicon native dev environment work properly?
- for getting the dependencies, the requirements are also declared in `python/requirements.txt`. maybe just use that, rather than using `ray-deps`?

I think at a high level, there's a better way to approach this. This solution still requires that we resolve all of the partition keys once, which seems needlessly expensive considering all we want to do is determine if this is a valid partition key or not.

In fact, in the case of TimeWindowPartitionsDefinitions (the impetus for this change), we already have an `is_valid_partition_key` method implemented, which does not require any partitions to be resolved.

My proposal would be:

1. make `is_valid_partition_key` a method on the base PartitionDefinition class (it can have a default implementation that just fetches all the partitions)
2. just call this `is_valid_partition_key` method to validate that we have a valid partition key, rather than doing partition = partitions_def.get_partition(...)

because this leaves you with a partition key (which is a string) instead of a `Partition` object, you'll need to use `partitioned_config.get_tags_for_partition_key` instead of `partitioned_config.get_tags_for_partition`. however, that implementation actually [just calls get_partition ](https://sourcegraph.com/github.com/dagster-io/dagster/-/blob/python_modules/dagster/dagster/_core/definitions/partition.py?L831)under the hood, so you'll need to rearrange those functions (really get_tags_for_partition should call get_tags_for_partition_key, as get_tags_for_partition only uses the key (partition.name) off of the partition anyway)

EDIT: Oops this is slightly wrong, as technically get_tags_for_partition does actually supply the full Partition object to self._tags_for_partition_fn. So an alternative approach (even simpler!) would be to just supply an efficient `get_partition` to override the default (slow) implementation on the TimeWindowPartitionsDefinition class.

This could basically just 1) check that it's a valid partition key, then 2) return a `Partition(partition_key, time_window_for_partition_key(partition_key))`

There are a few places which should be changed to make it look nice.
i.e. we simply select timestamp from the postgres and psyncopg2 converts it to the `datetime` object. Later, depending on our needs, we either convert it to the unixtime or use the isoformat() method to get the string. Last, but not least, `json.dumps(response, default=str)` will automatically convert datetime to string.

There are a couple of changes in the datetime formatting though. Microseconds are used instead of milliseconds and timezones showed as offsets instead of names, but IMO this is fine.

-        self._write_response(status_code, json.dumps(response), content_type='application/json')

aiohttp uses `pytest` with `pytest-aiohttp` plugin, not the standard `unittest`.
This is the deliberate choice. We don't accept tests that are `unittest` based.

P.P.S.
I'm aware of `unittest.IsolatedAsyncioTestCase` because I had added it to the CPython standard library.
It exists because CPython itself cannot depend on `pytest` project for running self-testing.
For third-party libs I always prefer pytest-style.

For my understanding, in the flow from the network through Plasma to building the DataFrame, where was the large memory inflation happening? My understanding of the flow is:
1. `ray.get()`: Plasma buffers are allocated and filled as objects are pulled, with the Arrow tables being deserialized directly into the Plasma buffers.
2. `builder.add_block()`: The block builder holds pointers to those Plasma buffers, so no new copies are made while add blocks to the builder.
3. `builder.build().to_pandas()`: The `.build()` and `.to_pandas()` calls create a copy or two during the Arrow table concatenation and the DataFrame construction, respectively.

This PR deals with stages (1) and (2) rather than the copy-heavy stage (3), and AFAICT wouldn't result in a lower peak during those two stages. Am I missing the creation of an extra copy or two in (1) and (2), maybe receiver-side queueing of byte chunks during pulls, or something else?

FYI, as long as direct downloads are working for most users, I'm not in a hurry to incorporate this into the main release.  

- I'm currently working on refactoring the network fetch code to make it better layered to use alternate access techniques, such as this and cloudscraper.  (cloudscraper is rather hacked in right now.)  And so that there's little or no cache-specific code in adapters.  Using the browser cache _as a cache_ before going out to the network is also an idea I'm thinking about, but it would need to take cache copy age into account.
 
- You've gone in the opposite direction I was thinking in dealing with the `_dk_...` keys.  You're reading the whole cache index to discard down to just the URLs.  I was thinking the opposite: figure out how they are keyed (it looks like it's just referring site?) and automatically add it.  The hard coded string was just a quick work around.

- Passing a reference to the adapter down into the cache ties the cache to one particular site/adapter.  If the whole index is being read, it would be nice not to for every story download.

- I think you may be overly optimistic about how well normalizing story and chapter URLs works in the current code.  Only a handful of adapters implement `normalize_chapterurl()`.  You may be correct, but I'll want to think about and review other adapters more first.

  You can quite reasonably argue that that contradicts what I just said about wanting to put the cache-reading code in as a more general layer.  It may require some changes to adapters and I expect that not all sites will be able to be supported by cache reading in the end.

- IMO, `map()` is forbidden.  The difference between py2 and py3 `map()` can break things in extremely subtle ways that have bitten me more than once.  If `next()` has the same py2/py3 differences, I don't want it either.  

- Right now, I'm thinking that the cache reading layer will need to be isolated in such a way that it will only be called (or even imported?) for py3 so the rest of FFF can still support py2 calibre.  If I end up having to choose between cache reading or continued py2 support, py2 is probably going to win.

I also made some specific code comments--I wrote those before this, so there's some duplication.

I think it's ok for now to have this convenient group based aliasing to make asset references easier to use. Users might want aliases for cross group references as well, but limiting this to just the current group and marking it experimental makes sense.

For a longer term solution, we should think about refactoring some code to separate the concept of an asset reference from an asset key. The main difference is that a `reference` will be resolved to a `key` at some point later in time. At asset definition time we should only store a reference (not a key, like we do currently) and define a specific point in time where all references are resolved to keys. I think this resolution logic should be scoped to the repository and unrelated to jobs.

My main concern is this can cause some regression by overloaded raylet. Ideally, we should also do backpressure from the raylet side to limit max # of concurrent lease requests, but I don't think we have time... 

I wonder if we can just do some CPU usage benchmarking and see how many lease requests ray can handle before being overloaded? I think we can do something like this ;

1. Set lease request very large (10 -> 500 -> 1K -> 10K -> 50K -> 100K for example)
2. Submit many tasks from a head node (maybe sth like 100K). 
3. Compare CPU usage of raylets among tests.

Another question is fairness. When we have 3 categories (A, B, C), 2 task each, do we submit tasks in 

1. A1, B1, C1, A2, B2, C2
2. or A1, A2, B1, B2, C1, C2
?

I think if it is 2, it can also has unexpected regression. I am just curious what's the current policy

I think this is okay as a fix for now, but I think in the long term we can actually move away from the lazy checkpointing logic + the marker.

Future checkpointing logic:
1. Worker pushes to persistent storage (driver, NFS, cloud are all the same).
2. Worker passes metadata to the coordinator. This is a lightweight `CheckpointMetadata` (<-- a made up class that's similar to today's `_TrackedCheckpoint`, except its data is always just a path of where the checkpoint lives, at the storage path).
3. Then, there's no need to distinguish between passing within a node vs. between nodes, since the metadata is so small.

sorry based on offline conversation with @fubuloubu -- @pandadefi since point of this PR is just to make it syntactically simpler to call ecrecover with an existing uint8 (without having to `convert()`) but the ecrecover precompile just expects 32 bytes, it would introduce inefficiency for use cases where the user has a `uint256` (since those are always checked to be in range for v anyways).

to address this, i think we should make ecrecover accept both kinds of input. in other words, `v` can be in `(uint8, uint256)` and r and s in `(bytes32, uint256)`. this maintains backwards compatibility while supporting the intent of this PR. the only downside is that it makes the API more confusing, but there is no EVM-level reason to pick one of these types over another.

> We're using the local storage to store downloaded Artifacts. Does Dagster always run with a local filesystem that we can use? And what would happen when there is no more disk space?

dagster is always running with a local filesystem, but when it's deployed in a remote setup, for example, each run could be in its own container which means local filesystem will be ephemera in a run scope, i.e. files downloaded to run 1's local filesystem won't be available to the next run.

i think it's ok to default to local filesystem, but it'd be great to give an option for users to download to other filesystems, e.g. an option to hook to an s3 bucket. -- but this isn't blocking this PR IMO. we can add it in a later iteration.

So I'm generally against this change due to opportunities for regressions and 'papering' over the bug.  Essentially charm-helpers 'owns' the file `.unit-state.db` as it predates the ops framework, and thus there are lots of charms *already* using that file.  If the ops framework needs an SQLite file, then it should have its own filename and not clobber the charm-helper's one (used in classic and reactive charms).  This is a hack, and it shouldn't be added to the library.

However, the Storage class initialise function *has* a path variable to allow multiple `Storage` objects to co-exist, so the ops framework could use.  Or it could set the `UNIT_STATE_DB` file.  Or the charm application code could do either of these actions, as the charm application code is bringing in charm-helpers.  If the ops framework is bring in charm-helpers then it shouldn't clobber the file itself.  I hope that makes sense.

Hi @benjello - would you be able to help me out here?

I've spent a while trying to get linear interpolation to work using LinearAverageRateTaxScale.
To simplify the problem I've created this test case to **just** test that there exists a set of brackets which allows us to correctly do linear interpolation.

For these brackets we test:
1) amounts **on** threshold values
2) amounts **between** threshold values (requiring interpolation)

The first assertion passes :heavy_check_mark:
However, the second assertion fails, with all result being near-misses of what is expected :x: 

Mathematically, I do not think there exists any set of brackets which would solve these constraints. To illustrate this mathematically, consider:
`f(2) == 10` :heavy_check_mark:  and `f(4) == 30` :heavy_check_mark:
But `f(3) != 20` :x: 

To satisfy the constraint for f(3) we must add another threshold at some point in the range 2<x<4. But then there will be a kink in the function `f` at that new threshold x. This cannot be the case because we know f(x) is strictly linear over the range 2<x<4.

 I'm really perplexed by this problem :confused: I suspect that maybe the `LinearAverageRateTaxScale.calc()` function simply isn't the right formula for this application?
 
 Biiiiig thanks in advance :+1: :pray:
